answer.text: A private placement of eligible securities by a listed company to Qualified Institutional Buyers (QIBs)
An issue of shares to the general public through book building
A placement of shares with retail investors at a discount
An offer of shares exclusively to foreign portfolio investors

explanation.text: **The Logic:** QIP is defined by SEBI ICDR Regulations as an issue of eligible securities by a listed issuer to QIBs (financial institutions, mutual funds, banks, etc.) on a private placement basis. The company must have been listed for at least 1 year before making a QIP. **The Trap:** QIP is not a public issue (option b) - it is specifically a private placement to institutional investors. A special resolution from shareholders is needed. **Cross-Exam Context:** A minimum of 10% of QIP securities must be allotted to mutual funds. Pricing follows SEBI's prescribed formula. Shares allotted can only be sold on a recognized stock exchange within 1 year. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** QIP is the fastest way for an already-listed company to raise equity from institutions. It has lighter regulatory requirements compared to a full public issue, though SEBI regulations still apply.
